## Summary
The zombie argument contends that philosophical zombies — beings physically identical to conscious humans but lacking subjective experience — are conceivable and therefore metaphysically possible. If zombies are possible, then consciousness is not entailed by physical properties, refuting physicalism.

## Evidence
The argument rests on the conceivability-possibility link: if zombies are conceivable without contradiction, they are possible in some metaphysically possible world. Physicalists respond either by denying conceivability (type-A: zombies are not genuinely conceivable) or by denying the conceivability-possibility link (type-B: conceivability does not entail possibility). The debate remains unresolved.

## Significance
The zombie argument is the most influential anti-physicalist argument in contemporary philosophy of mind. Its validity determines whether a purely physical explanation of consciousness is even possible in principle, shaping the entire research program in consciousness studies.
